g.data-narration: A file system organizes and manages data on storage devices. It defines how files are named, stored, accessed, and protected.

Every file has attributes or metadata stored by the OS: Name identifies the file, Type indicates its format, Size shows bytes, Location points to the disk address, Protection controls access rights, and Timestamps record creation and modification times.

The operating system provides fundamental file operations: Create allocates space, Open loads metadata into memory, Read and Write transfer data, Seek repositions the file pointer, Close releases resources, and Delete frees the file space.
g.text: Open() adds entry to Open-File Table with file pointer

g.data-narration: Files are classified by type using extensions or magic numbers. Executable files have dot-exe extension, text files use dot-txt, source code uses dot-c, object files use dot-obj, and image files use dot-jpg. The OS may use file type to determine how to process a file.
g.text: Magic numbers (e.g., 0x7F ELF) identify type internally

g.data-narration: There are three main file access methods. Sequential access reads records one after another like a tape. Direct or random access jumps to any block by its number. Indexed access uses an index table that maps keys to block positions for fast lookup.

The file system is structured in layers. Applications call the Logical File System which manages metadata. The File Organization Module translates logical blocks to physical blocks. The Basic File System issues I/O commands. I/O Control contains device drivers. At the bottom are the physical storage Devices.
